[2022-07-15 15:42] megasupp (mega-supp): In prismata we call a set RPS (rock paper scissors) when there are several strategies such that every strategy loses to at least 1 other strategy. for example Tatsu is a very strong attacker, and if one player gets several tatsus and has no problems defending they are probably going to win, however because tatsu requires such a huge tech commitment it's very weak to units that can quickly add pressure like grenade mech, but grenade mech isn't actually an efficient attacker, so if one player buys grenade mech, and other players goes for tarsiers, while defending the grenade mech the player with grenade mechs will find themselves behind and most likely lose
[2022-07-15 15:43] megasupp (mega-supp): in those cases both players want to make non-commital moves which most of the time means buying more drones
[2022-07-15 15:45] megasupp (mega-supp): Also note that at some point the RPS breaks and there emerges a strategy that's better than all other ones
[2022-07-15 15:47] megasupp (mega-supp): typically if both players econ up a lot, tatsus become the best strategy because you can afford more tech on high econ and can easily buy tatsus and defend at the same time
[2022-07-15 15:49] sano712 (sano): I wouldn't say that's an RPS set though, because Tatsu doesn't clearly beat anything there, and blue red doesn't clearly lose to anything there
[2022-07-15 15:50] megasupp (mega-supp): yeah this particular set doesn't really give me an impression that tatsu is a viable strategy
[2022-07-15 15:51] megasupp (mega-supp): I explained because someone asked about RPS
[2022-07-15 15:52] megasupp (mega-supp): usually you should start thinking about tatsus if there is either a good red sink or some good absorbers so that both players need to go high econ

[2022-07-25 08:27] sano712 (sano): Minmax is supposed to refer to a two stage optimisation problem where you're minimising the objective function in the first stage and maximising the second stage. Such as minimising the cost of something under the worst case uncertainty. Aka making robust decisions
[2022-07-25 08:27] sano712 (sano): In general, it's just misused as a way of saying 'I'm being a nerd at this game and carefully micromanaging all attributes' or something
[2022-07-25 10:47] amalloy: the algorithm is called minimax. minmaxing is a different term used by different people to mean a different thing
[2022-07-25 10:48] amalloy: i imagine they're related, but you can't say someone is *wrong* to use it to refer to something other than this algorithm

[2022-07-25 11:21] sano712 (sano): I'm not a computer scientist, but judging from Wikipedia I think it's essentially the same thing looked at from different lenses. I wouldn't say it is an algorithm, rather a framework for which we can develop algorithms?

> Minimax (sometimes MinMax, MM[1] or saddle point[2]) is a decision rule used in artificial intelligence, decision theory, game theory, statistics, and philosophy for minimizing the possible loss for a worst case (maximum loss) scenario.
[2022-07-25 11:23] sano712 (sano): I also didn't say it's _wrong_, just that it's a misnomer
[2022-07-25 11:23] sano712 (sano): Similar to how DC power flow has nothing to do with direct current, yet it's widely used in the power systems literature
[2022-07-25 11:24] sano712 (sano): I think it's safe to say that if you're just carefully choosing attributes for your RPG character to hit some soft caps or whatever, that's not really minmaxing, but I often see it used in that sense
